currency.coins: Five coins: copper (cp), silver (sp), electrum (ep), gold (gp), platinum (pp). 10cp = 1sp, 10sp = 1gp, 10gp = 1pp; electrum (5sp = 1ep) is rare and often mistrusted, originating from fallen empires.
currency.cost_of_living: A copper piece buys a candle or torch; a silver piece buys a flask of oil or a night in a poor inn; a gold piece buys a bedroll, 50 feet of rope, or a goat. An unskilled laborer earns 2sp per day; a skilled artisan can earn a gold piece. Common prices: mug of ale 4cp, loaf of bread 2cp, hunk of cheese 1sp, chunk of meat 3sp, pitcher of common wine 2sp, modest lodging 5sp per night. Trade goods hold their value: a chicken costs 2cp, a goat 1gp, a sheep 2gp, a pig 3gp, a cow 10gp, an ox 15gp.
currency.commerce: Commoners deal in copper and silver. Peasants mostly barter — paying taxes in grain and cheese rather than coin. Only merchants, adventurers, and professional hirelings commonly deal in coins; nobility trade in gold bars, legal rights, and land. Guilds, nobles, and royalty regulate trade; guilds set prices for the goods and services they control.

social_order.hierarchy: Society is hierarchical. Nobles own land, collect taxes, and wield political influence; common folk accommodate nobility and avoid their displeasure. Below the aristocracy: wealthy merchants and business owners, then comfortable skilled tradespeople and military officers, then the modest class — soldiers with families, laborers, students, priests, and hedge wizards. The poor include unskilled workers, peddlers, thieves, and mercenaries. The truly wretched have no home at all.
social_order.governance: Local lords or councils rule towns; larger cities may have noble courts, royalty, or merchant councils. Town guards and local militias enforce the law; justice is swift and practical — fines, stocks, exile, or execution — as long-term imprisonment is expensive. Trade guilds regulate artisans and wield significant political power in cities. Military organizations range from standing national armies to town guards, noble's private forces, and mercenary companies. Human institutions — temples, governments, libraries, and codes of law — preserve traditions across generations.
social_order.languages: Most commoners cannot read; news travels by word of mouth, travelers, and bards. Common is the most widely spoken language, used by humans and as a shared tongue among the races. Each people has its own language: Dwarvish, Elvish, Halfling, Gnomish, Orc, among others. Exotic tongues — Draconic, Infernal, Celestial, Sylvan — are known mainly to scholars and those who deal with such beings.

physical_world.travel: On foot: roughly 24 miles per day at normal pace on roads. A riding horse covers more ground; a draft horse or pony is slower but steadier. A donkey or mule costs 8gp, a pony 30gp, a riding horse 75gp, a warhorse 400gp. A cart costs 15gp, a wagon 35gp. Coach cabs charge 3cp per mile between towns, 1cp within a city. Road and gate tolls cost about 1cp. Ship's passage runs 1sp per mile. Stabling a horse costs 5sp per day, feed 5cp per day. Roads between towns are the safest routes; the wilderness beyond is genuinely dangerous.
physical_world.planes: The world sits on the Material Plane. Two echo planes press close: the Feywild, home of fey creatures and wild magic, beautiful and treacherous; and the Shadowfell, a dark mirror of the world, domain of shadow and dread. Common folk know these as the source of strange stories — travelers lost for years who return unchanged, shadowy figures at crossroads, patches of forest where the rules change. Beyond those, most people know only that other planes exist — heavens, hells, and stranger places — where gods, angels, and fiends dwell.

gods_and_afterlife.worship: The gods are real. Their existence is not a matter of belief — clerics channel divine power, temples receive answered prayers, and holy symbols glow with genuine light. Most people worship pragmatically: pray to Chauntea (harvest) when planting, Waukeen (trade) before market day, Tymora (luck) before a journey, Sune (love) before a courtship, and appease Talos (storms) when thunder rolls.
gods_and_afterlife.pantheon: Good-aligned gods: Tyr (justice), Helm (protection), Torm (duty), Ilmater (endurance), Lathander (dawn and renewal), Selûne (moon), Sune (beauty and love). Neutral gods: Oghma (knowledge), Gond (craft), Silvanus (wild nature), Mielikki (forests), Tempus (war), Kelemvor (the dead), Waukeen (trade), Mystra (magic). Evil gods are feared and appeased rather than loved: Bane (tyranny), Shar (darkness), Cyric (lies), Talos (storms), Umberlee (the sea), Bhaal (murder), Myrkul (decay). Each nonhuman people has patron deities: Moradin for dwarves, Corellon Larethian for elves, Yondalla for halflings, Garl Glittergold for gnomes, Gruumsh for orcs.
gods_and_afterlife.death: When someone dies, their soul passes on to be claimed by their god. The faithless face a grim fate. Resurrection is possible but requires costly diamonds and powerful magic — far beyond the reach of common folk. Only the wealthy and fortunate are ever brought back.

world_knowledge.magic: Magic is real but rare in daily life. Most commoners witness it only through the local cleric's healing prayers or a traveling wizard's minor tricks. Those who wield it are few: clerics draw power from their gods, druids from the natural world, wizards from years of study and spellbooks, sorcerers from something in their blood, warlocks from pacts with powerful beings, paladins from sacred oaths, bards through music and performance, rangers from attunement to the wild. A hedge wizard in a village might manage a few useful cantrips. In cities, finding someone to cast a healing spell or identify a strange object costs 10 to 50 gold pieces — a fortune for a common laborer. Higher magic is rarer still and more expensive; the caster may demand a service rather than coin. Magic items exist but are extraordinarily valuable — beyond the price of gold for most. A potion of healing costs 50gp. The Weave, maintained by the goddess Mystra, is the source of arcane magic; when it has been disrupted in the past, the consequences were catastrophic.

peoples.humans: Humans are the most common and widespread people, adaptable and ambitious, reaching adulthood in their late teens and rarely living a century. They build lasting cities and institutions but rise and fall quickly compared to the longer-lived races.
peoples.dwarves: Dwarves stand 4 to 5 feet tall, live about 350 years, and dwell in mountain kingdoms where they mine gems and forge metalwork of renowned quality. Clan is everything to a dwarf; they are stubborn, loyal, and slow to trust outsiders.
peoples.elves: Elves are graceful, live over 700 years, and favor woodland realms. They meditate for four hours rather than sleeping. High elves are scholarly; wood elves are reclusive; drow live deep underground and are feared on the surface.
peoples.halflings: Halflings stand about 3 feet tall, live around 150 years, and tend toward peaceful farming communities. They are cheerful, brave beyond their size, and remarkably lucky.
peoples.gnomes: Gnomes are 3 to 4 feet tall, live 350 to 500 years, and are insatiably curious — famed as tinkers, inventors, and gemcutters.
peoples.half_elves: Half-elves live around 180 years and move between human and elven society, often becoming diplomats or wanderers.
peoples.half_orcs: Half-orcs are strong and intense, reaching adulthood by 14 and rarely living past 75; they face prejudice but prove themselves through action.
peoples.dragonborn: Dragonborn stand over 6 feet tall, have scales and breath weapons tied to their dragon ancestry, and live by clan honor. They are uncommon and striking.
peoples.tieflings: Tieflings bear horns, tails, and other marks of ancient infernal pacts in their bloodline; they resist fire and endure widespread mistrust from the ignorant.

monsters_and_wilderness.common_threats: The wilds are dangerous. Beyond town walls and patrolled roads, threats are real and commonplace. Goblins raid outlying farms and ambush travelers on forest roads. Kobolds infest old mines and cave networks. Bandits prey on trade routes. Orc war bands surge from mountains and badlands to raid settlements. Gnolls — savage hyena-like raiders — destroy everything in their path. Ogres are dim-witted brutes, trolls regenerate from nearly any wound unless burned, and hobgoblins wage organized war.
monsters_and_wilderness.undead: Undead are universally feared: skeletons and zombies rise in cursed places or at a necromancer's command; ghosts haunt ruins; vampires lurk in ancient keeps.
monsters_and_wilderness.dragons: Dragons are the most legendary creatures — immensely powerful, intelligent, and ancient. Chromatic dragons (red, blue, green, black, white) are cruel and rapacious; metallic dragons (gold, silver, bronze, brass, copper) are said to be noble and sometimes benevolent.
monsters_and_wilderness.greater_threats: Giants dwell in remote peaks, hills, and glaciers — hill giants are ravenous and stupid, frost giants fierce and warlike, and others rarer still. Devils and demons both exist: devils from the Nine Hells are cunning and lawful, demons from the Abyss are chaotic and destructive. The Feywild spawns strange, beautiful, and capricious fey creatures that sometimes cross into the world.
